explanation.title: Repeating Actions with the for Loop

codeExample.title: Student Ranking Table
codeExample.explanation:
- `for (let i = 0; i < students.length; i++)` — `let i` starts at 0; the loop runs while `i` is less than the array length
- `students[i]` — array access by index; `i` goes 0, 1, 2... matching each element in order
- `s.score >= 90 ? "A" : s.score >= 75 ? "B" : "C"` — a chained ternary assigning a grade inline
- `s.name.padEnd(8)` — pads the name to 8 characters so all columns align neatly
- `for (const s of students)` — iterates over objects directly; `s` is each student object in turn
- `topStudent = s` — updates the reference when a higher score is found

exercise.title: Compute Sum and Count of Even Numbers
exercise.instructions:
Given the array numbers = [3, 8, 15, 4, 22, 7, 10, 1, 6], use a for...of loop to compute two things: the sum of all numbers in the array, and a count of how many numbers are even (divisible by 2 with no remainder). Print the total sum and the even count on separate labelled lines.

Expected output:
Sum: 76
Even count: 5
hints: 💡 A number is even if num % 2 === 0 — the modulo operator gives the remainder.
💡 Inside the loop, add if (num % 2 === 0) { evenCount++; } after the sum line.
💡 evenCount++ increments the counter by 1 each time an even number is found.

questions.question: What are the three parts of a for loop header separated by semicolons?
questions.explanation: A for loop header is: for (initialisation; condition; update). Initialisation runs once before the loop starts. The condition is checked before every iteration — the loop stops when it is false. The update runs after each iteration. For example: for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++).

questions.question: When is for...of preferred over a classic indexed for loop?
options: When you need the index of each element
When you only need the value of each element and do not need its index
Only when the array contains strings
for...of is always slower and should be avoided
questions.explanation: for...of is preferred when you only need each element's value and do not need to know its position. The syntax is cleaner and less error-prone. Use a classic for loop when you need the index (to access a parallel array, build a numbered list, or modify elements in place).
